did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper utilized an artificial intelligence model (CE-certified as part of the deepLive™ system) to generate a BCC probability score and heatmap from LC-OCT images for in vivo margin assessment of basal cell carcinoma. The AI was trained on 685,000 LC-OCT images and its performance was evaluated in a reader study with 43 dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes the development and application of a standardized 'BCC-One-Stop-Shop' workflow combining dermoscopy and LC-OCT for margin assessment. It presents original observational data from 50 lesions, details the methodology for in vivo and ex vivo imaging, and includes specific case examples to demonstrate feasibility. The authors explicitly state that performance results will be reported in a separate publication, indicating this is a primary study focusing on protocol development and initial feasibility rather than a review of existing literature.

title: Co-Localized Dermoscopy and LC-OCT for AI-Assisted Margin Assessment of Basal Cell Carcinoma: Development of a “BCC-One-Stop-Shop” Workflow
brief_summary: This study developed a standardized "BCC-One-Stop-Shop" workflow to enable in vivo margin assessment of basal cell carcinoma within a single patient visit. The protocol integrates wide-field dermoscopy with line-field confocal optical coherence tomography (LC-OCT) and an AI model trained on 685,000 LC-OCT images to generate real-time BCC probability scores. A co-localization algorithm maps LC-OCT acquisitions onto dermoscopy images, creating a color-coded visualization of tumor margins. The feasibility of this approach was preliminarily evaluated in an observational study of 50 lesions. Results indicated that the protocol is quick to apply, easy to learn, and helpful for determining initial tumor margins. The authors conclude that this method could reduce time, costs, and patient burden by potentially sparing additional excision steps in micrographic surgery.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses exclusively on the margin assessment of basal cell carcinoma. The AI model was trained on 685,000 LC-OCT images from 1154 lesions in 1047 patients, and the feasibility was evaluated in an observational study of 50 BCC lesions.

all_task_types.target: BCC probability score (presence of basal cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The AI model predicts a BCC probability score (expressed as a percentage from 0% to 100%) for each vertical LC-OCT image. This score represents the likelihood of BCC presence, effectively functioning as a binary classification task (BCC vs. no BCC) output as a continuous probability value. The model also generates a heatmap to highlight regions suspected of containing BCC-related features based on this classification.

datasets_used.name: AI Training Dataset (Fischman et al.)
datasets_used.description: A large dataset of LC-OCT images used to train the AI model for BCC probability scoring. Referenced as the source for the model's training data in the current study.

use.training: Used to train the AI model that generates BCC probability scores and heatmaps.
use.other: The model's performance was evaluated in a separate reader study involving 43 dermatologists on a test set of 200 equivocal BCC cases (details in Fischman et al. [31]).

datasets_used.name: Observational Cohort (BCC-One-Stop-Shop Feasibility Study)
datasets_used.description: A prospective observational study cohort used to evaluate the feasibility and workflow of the co-localized dermoscopy and LC-OCT system for margin assessment. The study involved 50 lesions.

use.testing: Used for feasibility evaluation of the 'BCC-One-Stop-Shop' workflow, including the co-localization algorithm and AI visualization.
use.other: The study was observational; no preoperative modification of surgical margins was performed. Results indicated the protocol was quick to apply and helpful for initial margin determination.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: BCC probability score (expressed as a percentage from 0% to 100%)

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It describes the development of a workflow and presents preliminary feasibility results from an observational study of 50 lesions without statistical comparison or inferential testing.

AI Model Training Dataset (Fischman et a.metric: Performance improvement in dermatologists' diagnostic accuracy (Sensitivity and Specificity) when using LC-OCT with AI assistance compared to clinical and dermoscopic examination alone
AI Model Training Dataset (Fischman et a.notes: The AI model was trained on 685,000 LC-OCT images from 1154 lesions in 1047 patients. Performance evaluation was conducted in a reader study with 43 dermatologists on a test set of 200 equivocal BCC cases. Specific model metrics (e.g., AUC, accuracy) were not reported in this paper; details are available in Fischman et al. [31].

Observational Cohort (BCC-One-Stop-Shop.metric: Workflow feasibility, application time, and qualitative assessment

BCC-One-Stop-Shop Workflow (LC-OCT + Co-.feasibility_assessment: Quick to apply, easy to learn, helpful for initial determination of BCC tumor margins, positive patient reception

Observational Cohort (BCC-One-Stop-Shop.notes: This was an observational feasibility study of 50 lesions. No preoperative modification of surgical margins was performed. Two representative cases were presented: Case 1 (5 mm lesion, shoulder) showed complete (R0) excision confirmed by histology; Case 2 (4 mm lesion, upper lip) showed incomplete (R1) excision between 5 and 9 o'clock, which was correctly anticipated by the AI-assisted LC-OCT system. Performance results (quantitative diagnostic accuracy) are stated to be reported in a separate publication.

architecture_used.main_model: Artificial intelligence model (CE-certified as part of the deepLive™ system extension)
architecture_used.pretraining: Trained on a dataset comprising 685,000 LC-OCT images acquired from 1154 lesions in 1047 patients. Specific model architecture details (e.g., backbone, layers) are not reported in this paper; further details are available in Fischman et al. [31].
architecture_used.input_size: Vertical LC-OCT images (specific pixel dimensions not reported for the AI input, though LC-OCT image dimensions are listed as 400 × 400 pixels in Table 1).

architecture_details.type: Deep learning model for binary classification (BCC vs. no BCC)
architecture_details.mechanism: Generates a BCC probability score (0-100%) for each vertical LC-OCT image. Generates a heatmap by applying the model to sub-regions (patches) within the LC-OCT image to highlight regions suspected of containing BCC-related features.
architecture_details.visualization: Outputs a color-coded heatmap overlaid on LC-OCT images and modulates the color of a marker on the co-localized dermoscopy image based on the BCC score (blue for 0% to yellow for 100%).

training_details.validation_method: Evaluated in a reader study involving 43 dermatologists on a test set of 200 equivocal BCC cases.
training_details.performance_metrics: Sensitivity improvement of +25.8 percentage points and specificity improvement of +16.8 percentage points compared to clinical and dermoscopic examination alone.

classification_or_output_head.thresholding: A threshold is applied to heatmap patches to keep only those above a certain score, suppressing patches below the threshold.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: AI model (CE-certified as part of the deepLive™ system extension)
preprocessing_techniques: Application of a thin layer of paraffin oil to optimize optical coupling for dermoscopy and LC-OCT imaging
Drying of color markings for 5–10 minutes to ensure color stability and avoid smudging
Co-localization algorithm using Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) and Random Sample Consensus (RANSAC) to register surface images to video-dermoscope images
Automatic stitching of overlapping dermoscopic images into a mosaic
Application of a threshold to heatmap patches to suppress scores below a certain level
conclusions: Pre- and postoperative margin mapping using LC-OCT imaging is effective and feasible for the assessment of basal cell carcinoma margins.

The integration of real-time co-localization of LC-OCT acquisitions onto dermoscopy images and AI-calculated BCC probability scores represents a meaningful and practical enhancement for routine clinical use.
The 'BCC-One-Stop-Shop' workflow, which combines in vivo and ex vivo margin mapping, is quick to apply (10–20 minutes), easy to learn, and helpful for the initial determination of tumor margins.
The method has the potential to reduce time, costs, resources, excision sizes, and patient burden by sparing additional excision steps in micrographic surgery.
Patients responded positively to the recognizable visualization of the disease provided by the AI-assisted system.
The protocol is applicable to both Mohs/slow Mohs surgery and standard surgical excision, potentially improving margin control and tissue preservation independent of the surgical technique.

Larger-scale studies are warranted and currently being conducted to further evaluate the efficacy, safety, and diagnostic accuracy of the BCC-One-Stop-Shop method compared to the current gold standard (Mohs micrographic surgery).

Technical limitations of ex vivo LC-OCT imaging include challenges with optical coupling due to uneven tissue surfaces, tissue folding, or trapped air bubbles at the tissue-glass interface, which may impair visualization of the deep margin.
important_remarks: This is a protocol development and feasibility study, not a definitive diagnostic accuracy study; quantitative performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUC) for the 'BCC-One-Stop-Shop' workflow are explicitly stated to be reported in a separate publication.
The study is observational; no preoperative modification of surgical margins was performed based on the LC-OCT/AI findings, limiting the assessment of clinical impact on patient outcomes.

The AI model was trained on a large dataset (685,000 images from 1154 lesions) but specific architectural details are not provided in this paper, referencing a separate study (Fischman et al.) for training and validation details.
The feasibility evaluation was limited to a small cohort of 50 lesions, which restricts the generalizability of the workflow's ease-of-use and time-efficiency claims.
Two representative cases were presented to illustrate the workflow, but these are anecdotal examples rather than a comprehensive statistical analysis of diagnostic concordance with histology.

Technical limitations of ex vivo LC-OCT imaging were noted, including challenges with optical coupling due to uneven tissue surfaces, folding, or trapped air bubbles, which may impair deep margin visualization.
Potential conflicts of interest exist, as several authors are employees of Damae Medical (the manufacturer of the deepLive™ system) or have received lecture fees/travel costs from the manufacturer.
The study was conducted at a single center (University Hospital Augsburg and associated clinics), raising concerns about external validation and potential site-specific bias.

The co-localization algorithm relies on SIFT and RANSAC, which may be sensitive to skin texture and lesion characteristics, though the paper claims robustness without providing error rates for failed co-localizations.
